scope.summary: Implement a reverse_seam authoring operation that swaps SeamSpec.from and SeamSpec.to endpoints on an existing draft seam, correcting inside/outside face orientation without re-authoring the full GarmentSpec
acceptance_criteria: The edit_seam MCP tool (or a dedicated reverse_seam variant) accepts (garment_id, seam_id, reverse: true) and swaps SeamSpec.from <-> SeamSpec.to, emitting a TailorPanelCrdtUpdateRecorded seam-mutation event
Reversing a seam with gather_ratio != 1.0 inverts the ratio (new_ratio = 1.0 / old_ratio) so the gather amount is preserved relative to the correct edge direction

The SimulationReceipt includes a seam_direction_hint field when the validator detects likely inside/outside inversion (cloth clumping at seam point), enabling the model self-correction loop to suggest a reverse_seam action
Round-trip test confirms reverse then reverse returns to original SeamSpec
The ModelManual documents reverse seam as the remedy for inside-out collar, sleeve, and pocket seam failures
scope.risk_if_missed: Seam orientation errors are the most common authoring mistake in MD — a collar appears on the wrong side of the neckline, a pocket bag folds outward, or a sleeve cap inverts. In MD this is a one-click fix. Without reverse_seam the only recourse is to re-author the affected SeamSpec from scratch, which breaks the iterative quality loop (MT-268) and makes model-authored garments fragile against orientation errors.

risks: Broad MT scope can hide multiple review targets.
Markdown MT lists may disagree with packet-declared active/next MT truth.
Receipt routing may target an old MT if contract identity is omitted.
minimum_controls: Exactly one primary proof target per MT unless the packet records why not.
MT identity must be stable and referenced by receipt correlation.
Generated projection drift must not wake Coder or Validator by itself.
